elements.sent-2411-001: Direction of arrival (DoA) estimation is a common sensing problem in radar, sonar, audio, and wireless communication systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: It has gained renewed importance with the advent of the integrated sensing and communication paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-003: To fully exploit the potential of such sensing systems, it is crucial to take into account potential hardware impairments that can negatively impact the obtained performance.
elements.sent-2411-004: This study introduces a joint DoA estimation and hardware impairment learning scheme following a model-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, a differentiable version of the multiple signal classification (MUSIC) algorithm is derived, allowing efficient learning of the considered impairments.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed approach supports both supervised and unsupervised learning strategies, showcasing its practical potential.
elements.sent-2411-007: Simulation results indicate that the proposed method successfully learns significant inaccuracies in both antenna locations and complex gains.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, the proposed method outperforms the classical MUSIC algorithm in the DoA estimation task.
elements.sent-2411-009: Direction of arrival (DoA) estimation refers to the angular estimation of a wavefront impinging on a sensor array.
elements.sent-2411-010: In modern wireless communication systems, DoAs are used for a wide variety of tasks: localization, target tracking, beamforming, or interference management~\cite{pesavento2023three,Corlay24 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The growing interest in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), where sensing and communication are jointly performed, has further reinforced the need of efficient DoA estimation.
elements.sent-2411-012: Classically, model-based algorithms have been proposed to solve the DoA estimation problem~\cite{Schmidt1986,Barabell83,VanVeen88,Roy1989,Vasylyshyn09 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: While the use of subspace methods such as multiple signal classification (MUSIC)~\cite{Schmidt1986 , or estimation of signal parameters via rotational invariance techniques (ESPRIT)~\cite{Roy1989 allows to increase estimation performance, such methods are sensitive to hardware impairments.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Several studies have been carried out to assess the impact of hardware impairments on wireless communication performance~\cite{Björnson13,Björnson14,Björnson15,Höhne10 ,chatelier_impact_2022 , but also on sensing performance~\cite{Bozorgi21,Tubail24,Chen24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Machine learning has recently emerged as an alternative to classical signal processing methods in many fields of wireless communication~\cite{OShea2017,Wang2017 , including beamforming~\cite{Alkhateeb2018 , channel estimation~\cite{Balevi2020 , localization~\cite{Chatelier2023b or decoding~\cite{Corlay2022 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, such ML approaches can be seen as black boxes, leading to minimal interpretability of the learned system.
elements.sent-2411-017: Alternatively, the model-based machine learning paradigm~\cite{Shlezinger23,Shlezinger23b offers increased interpretability in the learned structure, as models from signal processing are used to structure and initialize neural architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This paradigm has been employed in several communication problems: channel estimation~\cite{yassine2022,Chatelier2022efficient , precoding~\cite{Lavi23 , beam prediction~\cite{Yassine23 , detection~\cite{Samuel17 , and also in ISAC design~\cite{mateos2023model,Mateos_Ramos24 .. Contributions.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: It is proposed to tackle the joint DoA estimation and hardware impairments learning problem following the model-based machine learning paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this paper, the use of a differentiable MUSIC (diffMUSIC) algorithm for antenna array location inaccuracies and complex gains impairments learning is studied.
elements.sent-2411-021: This differentiable version of the classical MUSIC algorithm is achieved by replacing the non-differentiable argmax step by a convex combination of DoAs.
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, supervised and unsupervised learning strategies are presented.
elements.sent-2411-023: Experiments conducted on synthetic data against several baselines demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-024: It is shown that the proposed method is able to compensate for significant antenna array impairments.
elements.sent-2411-025: Specifically, impairments are considered on both the location and complex gain of each radiating elements composing the antenna array.. Related work.
elements.sent-2411-026: The use of machine learning methods in the DoA estimation context has been studied in the literature: a non-exhaustive list includes~\cite{Wu2019,Cong2021,Papageorgiou2021,Lan2023,Ji2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Additionally, the model-based machine learning paradigm has been applied to this task in~\cite{Shmuel2023a, Shmuel2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The MUSIC method considers several assumptions on the system model: e.g., sources must be non-coherent, the signals must be narrowband.
elements.sent-2411-029: In~\cite{Shmuel2023a, Shmuel2023 , the authors aim to relax these assumptions by introducing SubspaceNet: a convolutional neural network (CNN) that outputs a surrogate covariance matrix, that can then be used by the MUSIC method.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Such method has been shown to counter the effect of coherent sources in addition to hardware impairments.
elements.sent-2411-031: The diffMUSIC method proposed in this paper is aimed at achieving hardware impairments learning without the use of a CNN, with the benefit of drastically reducing the number of learnable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-032: A uniform linear array (ULA) composed of $N$ antennas with half-wavelength spacing is considered.
elements.sent-2411-033: The presented approach can be straightforwardly extended to any given antenna array geometry.
elements.sent-2411-034: Measurements are carried out over $T$ snapshots: \begin{equation}\label{eq:system_model} \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{N},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{C ^{N \times T}$ corresponds to the measured signals, and $\boldsymbol{\theta \in \left[-\pi/2,\pi/2\right]^M$ represents the sources DoAs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The narrowband sources signals are defined as $\mathbf{S = \left\{\mathbf{s _i\right\}_{i=1 ^T \in \mathbb{C ^{M \times T}$, such that $\mathbf{s _i \sim \mathcal{CN}\left(\mathbf{0 _M,\sigma^2_s \mathbf{I _M\right)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The sensing noise $\mathbf{N = \left\{\mathbf{n _i\right\}_{i=1 ^T \in \mathbb{C ^{N \times T}$ is defined such that $\mathbf{n _i \sim \mathcal{CN}\left(\mathbf{0 _N,\sigma^2_n\mathbf{I _N\right)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, $\boldsymbol{\zeta = \left[\left\{g_i\right\}_{i=1 ^N, \left\{p_i\right\}_{i=1 ^N\right]$ corresponds to a physical parametrization of the array manifold.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: This system model is visualized in Fig.~\ref{fig:system_model .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.8\columnwidth]{figs/system_model.pdf} \caption{System model: blue antennas are the physical antennas, black antennas represent the nominal antennas} \label{fig:system_model}
elements.sent-2411-039: The DoA estimation problem can be seen as follows: can the DoAs $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ be recovered from the received signal $\mathbf{X}$? Subspace methods.
elements.sent-2411-040: The eigenstructure of the received signal covariance matrix can be exploited to perform DoA estimation.
elements.sent-2411-041: Indeed, with non-coherent sources, the covariance matrix of the received signal expresses as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:cov_model} \mathbf{\Gamma}_{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\mathbf{\Gamma}_{\mathbf{S}}\mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}^\herm\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right) + \sigma^2_n \mathbf{I}_N,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{\Gamma _{\mathbf{S }$, the covariance matrix of $\mathbf{S}$, is diagonal.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Since \begin{equation}\mathrm{rank}\left(\mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\mathbf{\Gamma}_{\mathbf{S}}\mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}^\herm\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\right) = N,\end{equation} $\mathbf{A _{\boldsymbol{\zeta }\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\mathbf{\Gamma _{\mathbf{S }\mathbf{A _{\boldsymbol{\zeta ^\herm\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)$ admits only $N$ strictly positive eigenvalues.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, $\mathbf{\Gamma _{\mathbf{X }$ admits the following eigenvalue decomposition (EVD): \begin{equation}\label{eq:evd} \mathbf{\Gamma}_{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^\herm,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{U}$ is the matrix containing the eigenvectors and $\mathbf{\Lambda}$ is the diagonal matrix holding the eigenvalues on its diagonal.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Then, combining Eq.~\eqref{eq:cov_model and~\eqref{eq:evd yields the following results (see~\cite[Chapter 4, pp.159-164]{Stoica2005 for more details):.}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The eigenvalues ordered by decreasing amplitude are $\lambda_1, \cdots, \lambda_M, \lambda_{M+1 , \cdots, \lambda_{M+1}$, where $\lambda_{M+1 = \sigma^2_n$ has multiplicity $N-M$.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The eigenvectors span a space that can be decomposed into signal and noise subspaces: $\mathbf{U = \left[\mathbf{U _S, \mathbf{U _N\right]$, with $\mathbf{U _S \bot \mathbf{U _N$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: It is then clearly established that $\mathbf{A _{\boldsymbol{\zeta }\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right) \bot \mathbf{U _N$, leading to: \begin{equation}\label{eq:spectrum} \forall i \in \llbracket 1,M\rrbracket, \norm{\mathbf{U}_N^\herm \mathbf{a}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}\left(\theta_i\right)}{2}^2=0.\end{equation}. MUSIC.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The core of this method lies in Eq.~\eqref{eq:spectrum and is summarized in Algorithm~\ref{alg:MUSIC .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Since the inverse of Eq.~\eqref{eq:spectrum tends to infinity for the sources' DoAs, the MUSIC algorithm involves evaluating this inverse on an angular grid $\boldsymbol{\theta _g$ and identifying the sources' DoAs as the arguments of the peaks.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: It is important to note from Eq.~\eqref{eq:argmax_computation that DoA estimation performance depends on the array parametrization knowledge: imperfect knowledge of the antenna gains and locations leads to error in the MUSIC spectrum peak locations and amplitudes, thereby altering the estimated DoAs, as presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:MUSIC_hardware_impairments .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{MUSIC algorithm.} \label{alg:MUSIC} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Measured signals $\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{C}^{N\times T}$, number of sources $M$, angular grid $\boldsymbol{\theta}_g \in \mathbb{R}^{N_{\theta}}$, current array parametrization knowledge $\boldsymbol{\zeta}$. \State Compute the sample covariance matrix: \begin{equation} \hat{\mathbf{\Gamma}}_{\mathbf{X}} = \dfrac{1}{T}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^\herm \end{equation} \State Perform the EVD of the sample covariance matrix: \begin{equation} \hat{\mathbf{\Gamma}}_{\mathbf{X}} = \tilde{\mathbf{U}}\tilde{\mathbf{\Lambda}}\tilde{\mathbf{U}}^\herm \end{equation} \State Order the eigenvectors by decreasing eigenvalues amplitude, and perform subspace separation: $\tilde{\mathbf{U}} = \left[\tilde{\mathbf{U}}_S, \tilde{\mathbf{U}}_N\right]$ \State Compute the MUSIC spectrum along the angular grid: \begin{equation}\label{eq:music_grid} P_{MUSIC}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}_g \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\norm{\tilde{\mathbf{U}}_N^\herm \mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}_g\right)}{2}^2} \end{equation} \State Estimate the DoAs as the argument of the $M$ top-peaks: \begin{equation}\label{eq:argmax_computation} \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \argmax_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_g,M} P_{MUSIC}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}_g \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right) \end{equation} \Ensure Estimated DoAs: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \in \mathbb{R}^M$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figs/MUSIC_hardware_impairments.pdf} \caption{MUSIC performance under gain and location impairments: nominal, resp. physical, represents the spectrum without, resp. with, hardware impairment knowledge} \label{fig:MUSIC_hardware_impairments}
elements.sent-2411-051: As previously mentioned, the performance of the classical MUSIC algorithm deteriorates when considering hardware impairments.
elements.sent-2411-052: To address this issue, it is proposed to modify its structure to make it differentiable, enabling the learning of the array parametrization within the DoA estimation scheme.. Model-based differentiable architecture.
elements.sent-2411-053: Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is leveraged to solve: \begin{mini! {\boldsymbol{\zeta {\espo{\mathcal{L}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta , \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta }\left(\mathbf{X}\vert\boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)\right) {\left(\boldsymbol{\theta ,\mathbf{X}\right) \sim \mathcal{P _{\left(\boldsymbol{\theta ,\mathbf{X}\right) , \tag{$\mathrm{P 1$} { { \addConstraint{\boldsymbol{\zeta \in \mathbb{C ^N\times \mathbb{R ^N {\nonumber \end{mini! where $\mathcal{P _{\left(\boldsymbol{\theta ,\mathbf{X}\right)}$ represents the data distribution, $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function on the DoAs, $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ are the true DoAs associated to the measurements $\mathbf{X}$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta }\left(\mathbf{X}\vert\boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)$ are estimated DoAs from a given DoA estimator with array parametrization knowledge $\boldsymbol{\zeta}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In order to solve~\eqref{eq:optim_problem through SGD, it is necessary to compute the derivatives $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\zeta }\mathcal{L (\boldsymbol{\theta , \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta }\left(\mathbf{X}\vert\boldsymbol{\zeta}\right))$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Using the derivative chain rule, it can easily be shown that one has to compute $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\zeta \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta }\left(\mathbf{X}\vert\boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)$ to compute $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\zeta }\mathcal{L (\boldsymbol{\theta , \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta }\left(\mathbf{X}\vert\boldsymbol{\zeta}\right))$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: This hard decision scheme can be represented through angular masks containing a single value at each spectrum peak, with no operations performed within those windows.
elements.sent-2411-057: Since only the operations following the applications of the fixed angular masks are considered for gradient computations, it leads to the non-existence of $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\zeta \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta }\left(\mathbf{X}\vert\boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)$ for the MUSIC method, which results in its non-differentiability.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: It is thus proposed to replace this hard-decision scheme with a differentiable softmax-based approach outlined in Algorithm~\ref{alg:diffMUSIC , where each estimated DoA can be seen as a convex combination of grid DoAs around the associated MUSIC spectrum peak.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Note that $\boldsymbol{\theta ^{\mathrm{mask _i=\Pi_L(\boldsymbol{\theta _g,\theta_{i ^{\mathrm{peak )$ denotes a windowing operation of size $L$, applied on the DoA grid $\boldsymbol{\theta _g$, centered around $\theta_{i ^{\mathrm{peak }$ such that $\boldsymbol{\theta ^{\mathrm{mask _i \in \mathbb{R ^L$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: As for MUSIC, the diffMUSIC method initially estimates the spectrum peaks using a non-differentiable peak finding method: $\argmax$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:peak_finding_diffMUSIC .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Then, angular windows are defined around each peaks.
elements.sent-2411-062: As depicted in Eq.~\eqref{eq:diff_DoA_est , within each window, the estimated DoAs are computed using differentiable operations involving the steering matrix $\mathbf{A _{\boldsymbol{\zeta }\left(\boldsymbol{\theta _{g}\right)$, through the computed MUSIC spectrum.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This ensures that $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\zeta \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta }\left(\mathbf{X}\vert\boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)$ exists for a fixed angular mask, thereby making the method differentiable.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Note that, in addition to being differentiable, this approach also increases angular resolution as off-grid estimates are possible through the convex DoA combination.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{diffMUSIC algorithm.} \label{alg:diffMUSIC} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Computed MUSIC spectrum $P_{MUSIC}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}_g \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)$ from Eq.~\eqref{eq:music_grid}, angular window size $L$, current array parametrization knowledge $\boldsymbol{\zeta}$. \State Find peaks in $P_{MUSIC}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}_g \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)$: $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\mathrm{peak}} = \{\theta_{i}^{\mathrm{peak}}\}_{i=1}^M$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:peak_finding_diffMUSIC} \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\mathrm{peak}} = \argmax_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_g,M} P_{MUSIC}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}_g \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right) \end{equation} \ForEach{$\theta_{i}^{\mathrm{peak}}$} \State Compute an angular mask centered around $\theta_{i}^{\mathrm{peak}}$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:angular_mask} \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\mathrm{mask}}_i = \Pi_L(\boldsymbol{\theta}_g,\theta_{i}^{\mathrm{peak}}) \end{equation} \State Estimate the associated DoA: \begin{equation}\label{eq:diff_DoA_est} \hat{\theta}_i = \left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\mathrm{mask}}_i\right)^\transp softmax\left(P_{\mathrm{MUSIC}}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\mathrm{mask}}_i \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)\right) \end{equation} \EndFor \Ensure Estimated DoAs: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \in \mathbb{R}^M$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-065: As presented in~\cite{Shmuel2023a,Shmuel2023 , the root mean periodic squared error (RMPSE) on the DoAs can be used as a loss function to solve~\eqref{eq:optim_problem in a supervised learning (SL) manner.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: This loss function is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:RMPSE_loss} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SL},\theta} = \dfrac{1}{\abs{\mathcal{T}}} \sum_{\left(\boldsymbol{\theta},\mathbf{X}\right) \in \mathcal{T}} \min_{\mathbf{P} \in \mathcal{P}} \frac{\norm{\mathrm{mod}_{\pi} \left(\boldsymbol{\theta}-\mathbf{P}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\left(\mathbf{X}\vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)\right)}{2}}{\sqrt{M}},\end{equation} where $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of permutation matrices, and $\mathcal{T = \left\{\boldsymbol{\theta _i, \mathbf{X _i\right\}_{i=1 ^{N_t}$ is the training set.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The RMPSE takes into account the permutation invariance in the DoA learning task through the permutation matrices $\mathbf{P \in \mathcal{P}$: more details can be found in~\cite{Shmuel2023a .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Rather than learning on estimated DoAs, a proposed alternative strategy is to maximize the MUSIC spectrum amplitude at the true DoA locations.
elements.sent-2411-069: The associated supervised loss is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SL},P} = -\dfrac{1}{\abs{\mathcal{T}}} \sum_{\left(\boldsymbol{\theta},\mathbf{X}\right) \in \mathcal{T}}\sum_i P_{\mathrm{MUSIC}}\left(\theta_i \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: This approach does not directly solve~\eqref{eq:optim_problem ; instead, it addresses a proxy problem, the solution of which translates into minimizing the DoA estimation error.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Indeed, minimizing $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$ can be interpreted as finding an array parametrization that maximizes the orthogonality between steering vectors associated to the true DoAs and the noise subspace.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Once the impairments are learned using $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$, the DoAs can then be estimated using the classical MUSIC algorithm, or through diffMUSIC, with the learned array.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: This alternative SL strategy presents a reduced computational complexity during training in comparison to the RMPSE based strategy.
elements.sent-2411-074: This is presented in Table~\ref{table:time_complexity , where $\kappa_{\mathrm{peak }$, resp.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: This complexity reduction arises from the fact that, with $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$, the MUSIC spectrum only needs to be evaluated at the true DoA locations, rather than over a fine angular grid, as required for computing the DoAs with $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,\theta}$.. Unsupervised learning.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The proposed SL approaches rely on true DoA knowledge, which may limit their practical applicability.
elements.sent-2411-077: An unsupervised learning (UL) strategy can be implemented by using a loss function designed to maximize the MUSIC spectrum peaks within each angular mask of diffMUSIC.
elements.sent-2411-078: Such loss function can, for instance, be based on the coefficient of variation, the Gini coefficient, the kurtosis or the Kullback-Leibler divergence relative to the uniform distribution.
elements.sent-2411-079: It is proposed to consider a Jain's index (JI) based loss function where minimization translates to a sharp peak inside the masked spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-080: The JI is defined as: \begin{equation}\forall \mathbf{x}\in \mathbb{R}^n, \text{ } \mathfrak{J}\left(\mathbf{x}\right) = \dfrac{\left(\sum_i x_i\right)^2}{n\sum_i x_i^2},\end{equation} and the associated unsupervised loss function is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{UL}} = \dfrac{1}{\abs{\mathcal{T}}} \sum_{\mathbf{X} \in \mathcal{T}} \sum_i \mathfrak{J}\left(P_{\mathrm{MUSIC}}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\mathrm{mask}}_i\left(\mathbf{X} \vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)\vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)\right),\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{\theta ^{\mathrm{mask _i\left(\mathbf{X}\vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)$ is the obtained angular mask for given measurements in Eq.~\eqref{eq:angular_mask .}}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{tabular}{cccc} $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SL},\theta}$ $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SL},P}$ \makecell{Time complexity} $\mathcal{O}\left(N N_{\theta} + \kappa_{\mathrm{peak}} + \kappa_{\mathrm{EVD}}\right)$ $\mathcal{O}\left(N M + \kappa_{\mathrm{EVD}}\right)$ \end{tabular} \caption{Training complexity comparison: $M \ll N_{\theta}$} \label{table:time_complexity}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{minipage}{\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figs/combined_params_comparison.pdf} \caption{Learned parameters comparison for $M=5$, $30$dB SNR} \label{fig:learned_params} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figs/spectrum_comparison_10_dB_id_30.pdf} \caption{MUSIC spectrums with learned arrays, $M=5$, $T=100$, $10$dB SNR} \label{fig:learned_spectrums} \end{minipage}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{minipage}{\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figs/RMPSE_vs_SNR.pdf} \caption{RMPSE evolution with sensing SNR, $T=100$} \label{figs:rmpse_vs_SNR} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figs/RMPSE_vs_T.pdf} \caption{RMPSE evolution with snapshot number, $30$dB SNR} \label{figs:rmpse_vs_T} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-081: It is proposed to study the impairments learning performance of the proposed diffMUSIC method against several baselines and in different scenarios.. Dataset generation.
elements.sent-2411-082: A ULA with $N=16$ antennas is considered.
elements.sent-2411-084: The sources DoAs are generated as $\forall i \in \llbracket 1,M\rrbracket, \theta_i \sim \mathcal{U}\left[-80^\circ, 80^\circ\right]$.
elements.sent-2411-085: The sensing signal to noise ratio (SNR) is computed as $\mathrm{SNR = 10\log_{10}\left(\sigma^2_s/\sigma^2_n\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Concerning the hardware impairments, imperfections in both antenna locations and gains are taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-087: For the antenna locations, one has: \begin{equation}\forall i \in \llbracket 1, M\rrbracket, p_i = \tilde{p}_i + \delta_{p_i},\end{equation} where $p_i$, resp.
elements.sent-2411-088: Note that the locations are only altered along the non-null dimension of the ULA.
elements.sent-2411-089: Similarly, for the antenna gains, one has: \begin{equation}\forall i \in \llbracket 1, M \rrbracket, g_i = \tilde{g}_i + \delta_{g_i},\end{equation} where $g_i$, resp.
elements.sent-2411-090: Unless otherwise stated, $M=5$ sources, $T=100$ snapshots, $\eta=0.5 \lambda/2$ and $\sigma^2_{g = 0.36$ are considered.. Baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The proposed approach is compared against the classical MUSIC algorithm (denoted as $M$) with nominal and physical array, but also against diffMUSIC (denoted as $dM$) with physical array.
elements.sent-2411-092: Note that the window size $L$ of diffMUSIC is optimized in each considered scenario.
elements.sent-2411-093: Additionally, it is proposed to compare diffMUSIC against the SubspaceNet network presented in~\cite{Shmuel2023 : as its goal is to learn a surrogate covariance matrix to counter imperfections in the sensing process, it is able to take into account hardware impairments.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The evaluation metric for all approaches is the RMPSE defined in Eq.~\eqref{eq:RMPSE_loss evaluated over a test set.. Performance against baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Table~\ref{table:perf_baselines presents a DoA estimation performance comparison for $M=\left\{1,5\right\}$ sources, at $30$dB SNR.}
elements.sent-2411-096: It can be seen that the proposed approaches outperform the MUSIC algorithm with nominal array knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-097: Moreover, one can see that SubspaceNet performs relatively well for $M=1$ but totally fails to learn the imperfections in the multi-source scenario.
elements.sent-2411-098: Additionally, it can be seen that diffMUSIC with $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$ equals or outperforms diffMUSIC with physical array, indicating good impairment learning capabilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Furthermore, one can remark that the UL approach presents good performance in the multi-sources scenario: this is of particular interest as this approach does not rely on labeled DoAs.
elements.sent-2411-100: Note that this approach could be extended in an online learning strategy where the antenna array would learn its hardware impairments on the fly.
elements.sent-2411-102: It is proposed to visualize the learned parameters by the proposed methods for $M=5$ and their impact on the associated MUSIC spectrums.
elements.sent-2411-103: Fig.~\ref{fig:learned_params presents the learned parameters: the center of each circle represents an antenna location, the circle radius represents the associated complex gain magnitude, and the segment angle represents the associated complex gain phase.}
elements.sent-2411-104: It can be seen that the $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,\theta}$ approach presents good impairments learning except for two antennas whose defaults are small.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: It is worth noting that both the $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$ and $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{UL }$ approaches achieve near-perfect learning of the impairments which aligns with their strong performance observed in Table~\ref{table:perf_baselines .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Fig.~\ref{fig:learned_spectrums presents the spectrums obtained with the learned parameters by the different approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Results are illustrated at $10$dB SNR for better visualization.
elements.sent-2411-108: One can remark that, as expected by its definition, the spectrum obtained with the array parametrization learned through $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$ tightly follows the physical spectrum.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: One can also note that the peaks of the spectrum obtained through $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,\theta}$ are aligned with the ones of the physical spectrum.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Unsurprisingly, an angular shift is observed in the peaks of the spectrum obtained through $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{UL }$: while this approach allows to maximize peak amplitudes, its unsupervised nature does not allow to add angular bias on the peak locations during training.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: This angular shift phenomenon is particularly noticeable near the endfire of the antenna array.
elements.sent-2411-113: Fig.~\ref{figs:rmpse_vs_SNR presents the DoA estimation performance evolution with variable SNR, for $M=5$ sources and $T=100$ snapshots.}
elements.sent-2411-114: One can remark that, while the $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,\theta}$ approach presents good performance in the high SNR region, the $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$ approach presents the overall best performance as it follows tightly the diffMUSIC performance with physical array.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: It is worth noting that, in the very low SNR region, both the $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,\theta}$ and $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{UL }$ approaches are significantly affected by noise, preventing them from effectively learning hardware impairments.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: In contrast, the $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$ approach does not suffer from that issue.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Fig.~\ref{figs:rmpse_vs_T presents the DoA estimation performance evolution with variable number of snapshots for $M=5$ sources and $30$dB SNR.}
elements.sent-2411-119: As expected, the estimation performance degrades across all approaches when the number of snapshots is low.
elements.sent-2411-120: This can be attributed to the higher estimation error in the empirical covariance matrix when $T$ is low.
elements.sent-2411-121: It is also worth noting that the $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{SL ,P}$ approach exhibits the overall best performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: It demonstrates its superiority against other methods as it offers both better estimation performance in low SNR or low snapshot scenarios, while also achieving lower time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-123: This paper discussed the application of a differentiable version of the MUSIC algorithm for learning hardware impairments in the DoA estimation context.
elements.sent-2411-124: The differentiable MUSIC algorithm was obtained by replacing the non-differentiable argmax search by a softmax-based approach, where each estimated DoA can be viewed as convex combination of DoAs.
elements.sent-2411-125: Additionally, both supervised and unsupervised loss functions have been proposed for this impairment learning task.
elements.sent-2411-126: The proposed methods have been evaluated against several baselines, showing their good hardware impairment learning and DoA estimation abilities.
elements.sent-2411-127: Specifically, it has been shown that the proposed methods are able to learn hardware impairments even in the presence of high sensing noise and low number of measurement snapshots.
elements.sent-2411-128: Furthermore, the unsupervised learning strategy is of particular interest as it enables the antenna system to perform hardware impairment compensation without requiring measured DoAs.
elements.sent-2411-129: Future work will focus on extending the differentiable MUSIC approach to address coherent or partially correlated sources, and its application to near-field localization.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}

elements.sent-2411-130: Since \begin{equation}\mathrm{rank}\left(\mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\mathbf{\Gamma}_{\mathbf{S}}\mathbf{A}_{\boldsymbol{\zeta}}^\herm\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\right) = M,\end{equation} $\mathbf{A _{\boldsymbol{\zeta }\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)\mathbf{\Gamma _{\mathbf{S }\mathbf{A _{\boldsymbol{\zeta ^\herm\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)$ admits only $M$ strictly positive eigenvalues.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: As presented in~\cite{Shmuel2023a,Shmuel2023 , the root mean squared periodic error (RMSPE) on the DoAs can be used as a loss function to solve~\eqref{eq:optim_problem in a supervised learning (SL) manner.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: This loss function is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:RMSPE_loss} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SL},\theta} = \dfrac{1}{\abs{\mathcal{T}}} \sum_{\left(\boldsymbol{\theta},\mathbf{X}\right) \in \mathcal{T}} \min_{\mathbf{P} \in \mathcal{P}} \frac{\norm{\mathrm{mod}_{\pi} \left(\boldsymbol{\theta}-\mathbf{P}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\left(\mathbf{X}\vert \boldsymbol{\zeta}\right)\right)}{2}}{\sqrt{M}},\end{equation} where $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of permutation matrices, and $\mathcal{T = \left\{\boldsymbol{\theta _i, \mathbf{X _i\right\}_{i=1 ^{N_t}$ is the training set.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The RMSPE takes into account the permutation invariance in the DoA learning task through the permutation matrices $\mathbf{P \in \mathcal{P}$: more details can be found in~\cite{Shmuel2023a .}}
elements.sent-2411-134: This alternative SL strategy presents a reduced computational complexity during training in comparison to the RMSPE based strategy.
elements.sent-2411-135: Such loss function can for instance, be based on the coefficient of variation, the Gini coefficient, the kurtosis or the Kullback-Leibler divergence relative to the uniform distribution.
